meta.name: Qotbnama
meta.description: An educational tool to discover your approximate political orientation and learn about political systems and ideologies in a neutral way.

landing.title: Discover your political orientation
landing.subtitle: Take a short, research-based questionnaire to see where your political thinking sits on the map — and learn about the many systems and ideologies along the way.

neutral.title: Completely neutral
neutral.desc: Questions are built on established methodologies and never judge who you are.

private.title: Fully private
private.desc: No data is collected. Everything is processed locally on your own device.

educational.title: Engaging learning
educational.desc: After the quiz, explore political concepts through interactive cards and visuals.

visual.title: Visual analysis
visual.desc: Your result is shown clearly with a political compass, a radar chart and summary cards.

one.title: Answer
one.desc: Respond to 35 statements on a seven-point scale.

two.title: See your result
two.desc: Find your position on the political map and charts.

three.title: Learn
three.desc: Explore the ideas closest to you — neutrally and clearly.

landing.disclaimer: This is an educational estimate, not a scientific diagnosis.

intro.title: Before you begin
intro.lead: Qotbnama helps you understand your approximate political orientation neutrally and learn about a range of ideas.

purpose.title: Purpose
purpose.desc: The goal is educational: to help you understand political concepts and see your own position in political debate more clearly.

duration.title: Time needed
duration.desc: About 5 to 7 minutes — 35 statements, each answered on a seven-point scale.

privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.desc: No name, email or personal data required. Answers stay on your device and are never sent anywhere.

nature.title: Educational nature
nature.desc: This quiz is a scientific-educational exercise designed to encourage reflection, not to label anyone.

intro.disclaimer: This is an educational estimate and not a scientific or psychological diagnosis. Your result is only an approximate snapshot of your tendencies at the moment of answering.

questionnaire.mustAnswer: Please answer this question to continue.
questionnaire.incompleteWarning: {{count}} question(s) left unanswered. You can continue, but answering all of them improves accuracy.

questions.q01: The government should act to reduce the income gap between the rich and the poor.
questions.q02: Free competition and private enterprise are the best way to create growth and prosperity.
questions.q03: It is fair to tax the wealthy more in order to fund public services.
questions.q04: People's success or failure depends mainly on their own effort.
questions.q05: Maintaining order and security in society matters more than some individual freedoms.
questions.q06: People should be free to live differently from others, even if others disapprove.
questions.q07: Respect for authority and obedience to the law are core values of a healthy society.
questions.q08: Protecting citizens' privacy is more important than expanding state surveillance for security.
questions.q09: Living under a system whose leaders are chosen through free elections is very important.
questions.q10: Having a strong leader who can decide freely without worrying about elections benefits the country.
questions.q11: A free press and an independent judiciary are essential for a good society.
questions.q12: The rights of minority groups should be protected even against the will of the majority.
questions.q13: The government is responsible for ensuring a decent standard of living for all citizens.
questions.q14: The economy works better when the government intervenes and regulates less.
questions.q15: Key industries such as water, electricity and railways are better kept in public ownership.
questions.q16: Regulating businesses to protect people and the environment is necessary.
questions.q17: The country's laws should be based on religious principles and values.
questions.q18: Religion and government are better kept separate from each other.
questions.q19: Preserving long-standing traditions matters more than embracing new social change.
questions.q20: Policy should be based above all on scientific evidence and reason.
questions.q21: Society's wealth should be distributed more fairly among all people.
questions.q22: Lower taxes and a smaller government are good for the economy.
questions.q23: Workers' share of company profits matters more than the profits of owners.
questions.q24: Harsh punishments are the best way to reduce crime in society.
questions.q25: Adults should be free to make any choices in their private lives, as long as no one else is harmed.
questions.q26: When security is threatened, temporarily limiting some freedoms is acceptable.
questions.q27: As long as things go well, it doesn't much matter how or by whom decisions are made.
questions.q28: All citizens should be able to express their political views freely, even against those in power.
questions.q29: In a crisis, the military or experts should decide instead of elected politicians.
questions.q30: People should be more responsible than the government for meeting their own needs (such as pensions and healthcare).
questions.q31: The government should control the prices of essential goods to protect people.
questions.q32: Transferring state-owned companies to the private sector usually benefits society.
questions.q33: Teaching religious values should be part of the public school curriculum.
questions.q34: Laws on family and morality should be set by public consensus, not by religious rulings.
questions.q35: Preserving society's traditional values and identity should be a priority for the state.

economic.name: Economic
economic.description: Your view of markets, income equality and ownership in the economy.

social.name: Social
social.description: How you balance individual freedom against social order and authority.

democratic.name: Democratic
democratic.description: Your commitment to elections, press freedom and minority rights.

state.name: Role of the state
state.description: Your preference between a free market and state involvement in welfare.

secular.name: Religion & state
secular.description: Your view of religion's place in law-making and public life.

results.title: Your result
results.subtitle: An approximate picture of your political orientation at the moment of answering.
results.compassHint: Horizontal: economic (left ↔ right) — Vertical: social (libertarian ↔ authoritarian)
results.whyDesc: Your result comes from the sum of your answers. Each answer is weighted by the importance and direction of its question within the relevant dimension.
results.contributorsDesc: These questions — whether you agreed or disagreed — had the biggest impact on your position. Your answer is shown below each question:
results.strengthsDesc: Dimensions where you showed a clearer tendency.
results.limitsDesc: This is an educational estimate and cannot capture the full complexity of your views. Answers may change with time and context.
results.recommendedDesc: These concepts are close to your current position; exploring them rounds out your understanding.
results.noResultDesc: Complete the questionnaire first to see your result.

quadrantDesc.centrist: Your views balance between the various poles, sitting close to the center.
quadrantDesc.libertarian-left: You value economic equality alongside personal freedoms.
quadrantDesc.libertarian-right: You value free markets together with personal freedoms.
quadrantDesc.authoritarian-left: You lean toward economic equality with a stronger role for order and authority.
quadrantDesc.authoritarian-right: You lean toward free markets with an emphasis on social order and authority.

education.title: Political education
education.subtitle: Learn the key concepts of politics in plain, neutral language.

democracy.title: Democracy
democracy.summary: Government whose power derives from the votes of the people.
democracy.definition: Democracy is a system in which citizens, directly or through elected representatives, take part in public decisions, and political power draws its legitimacy from the people's vote.
democracy.history: The word goes back to ancient Athens (5th century BCE). Its modern form, representative democracy, spread from the 18th and 19th centuries after the American and French revolutions.
advantages: Rulers are accountable to the people
Citizens' rights and freedoms are protected
criticisms: Risk of a 'tyranny of the majority' over minorities
misconceptions: Democracy means only voting; in fact minority rights and a constitution are part of it too.
Democracy always guarantees the best decision; in practice it only guarantees the process of participation.

republic.title: Republic
republic.summary: A system whose head of state is not hereditary and is usually elected.
republic.definition: A republic is a form of government in which power rests with the people or their representatives, and the head of state is chosen rather than being a hereditary monarch.
republic.history: Early examples appear in ancient Rome and the Italian city-states. The modern concept took shape with the American (1776) and French (1792) revolutions.
advantages: No hereditary hierarchy at the top of power
Emphasis on citizenship and the rule of law
criticisms: Being a republic alone does not guarantee freedom
It can turn into a one-party or authoritarian system
misconceptions: Every republic is democratic; in fact some republics hold no free elections.
Republic and democracy are the same; the two concepts are actually distinct.

constitutional-monarchy.title: Constitutional monarchy
constitutional-monarchy.summary: A monarch whose power is limited by a constitution.
constitutional-monarchy.definition: In a constitutional monarchy the king or queen symbolizes the nation, but real power lies with parliament and an elected government, and a constitution limits the monarch's authority.
constitutional-monarchy.history: It began with England's Glorious Revolution (1688) and the Bill of Rights (1689) and became a model for many countries.
advantages: Ceremonial role separated from executive power
A link between tradition and democracy
criticisms: A hereditary post is seen as conflicting with equality
The cost of maintaining the monarchy
misconceptions: The monarch holds real executive power; in most of these systems the role is ceremonial.

absolute-monarchy.title: Absolute monarchy
absolute-monarchy.summary: A monarch in whom power is concentrated and unchecked.
absolute-monarchy.definition: In an absolute monarchy, all or most political power rests with the monarch, and elected institutions or a constitution place no serious limit on them.
absolute-monarchy.history: It was common in 17th- and 18th-century Europe; Louis XIV of France, with his phrase 'I am the state,' is a symbol of it.
criticisms: Lack of accountability and checks on power
Risk of tyranny and rights violations
misconceptions: Absolute monarchy belongs to the past; yet examples of it still exist.

parliamentary.title: Parliamentary system
parliamentary.summary: The executive emerges from, and is accountable to, parliament.
parliamentary.definition: In a parliamentary system the head of government (prime minister) is chosen from among members of parliament, and the government stays in office only as long as it holds parliament's confidence.
parliamentary.history: This model evolved in Britain and spread to many European and Commonwealth countries.
advantages: Coordination between the legislature and executive
An ineffective government can be removed by a confidence vote
criticisms: Concentration of power in the majority party
misconceptions: The prime minister is elected directly like a president; usually they are not.

presidential.title: Presidential system
presidential.summary: The president is elected separately and heads the executive.
presidential.definition: In a presidential system the president is elected separately, serves as both head of state and head of government, and the executive operates independently of the legislature.
presidential.history: Its model is the United States Constitution (1787), which emphasizes the separation of powers.
advantages: Direct election of the executive leader
criticisms: Risk of deadlock between president and parliament
Concentration of power in one person
misconceptions: Every country with a president is presidential; some are semi-presidential or parliamentary.

authoritarianism.title: Authoritarianism
authoritarianism.summary: Concentrated power and restricted political freedoms.
authoritarianism.definition: Authoritarianism is a system in which power is concentrated in one person or a small group, and political participation and civil liberties are heavily restricted.
authoritarianism.history: Examples were numerous in the 20th century, spanning a wide range from military to one-party regimes.
criticisms: Corruption due to a lack of oversight
examples: Numerous historical cases in Asia, Africa and Latin America
misconceptions: Authoritarianism is the same as totalitarianism; but totalitarianism is more extreme and all-encompassing.

totalitarianism.title: Totalitarianism
totalitarianism.summary: Total state control over every aspect of life.
totalitarianism.definition: Totalitarianism is an extreme form of authoritarianism in which the state seeks total control over every dimension of public and private life, from the economy to thought and culture.
totalitarianism.history: The concept was used especially to describe certain 20th-century regimes in the 1930s and 1940s.
advantages: (Analytically) large-scale mobilization of resources for state goals
criticisms: Complete elimination of freedom and pluralism
examples: Historical cases studied in political science
misconceptions: Every strict government is totalitarian; totalitarianism has specific, all-encompassing features.

liberalism.title: Liberalism
liberalism.summary: Emphasis on individual freedom, rights and the rule of law.
liberalism.definition: Liberalism is a body of thought that emphasizes individual freedom, equality before the law, human rights and limits on the power of the state.
liberalism.history: It grew out of the 18th-century Enlightenment and thinkers such as John Locke, and shaped the American and French revolutions.
advantages: Rule of law and equal rights
criticisms: Emphasis on the individual can come at the cost of social solidarity
examples: Liberal democracies in Europe and North America
misconceptions: Liberalism means 'left' in everyday American usage; but in its classical sense it also emphasizes free markets.

conservatism.title: Conservatism
conservatism.summary: Valuing tradition, stability and gradual change.
conservatism.definition: Conservatism is a tendency that emphasizes preserving established institutions and values, social stability, and measured, gradual change.
conservatism.history: It took shape as a reaction to the French Revolution; Edmund Burke is one of its first theorists.
examples: Conservative parties in the UK and Germany
misconceptions: Conservatism means opposing all change; in fact it emphasizes gradual change.

socialism.title: Socialism
socialism.summary: Emphasis on collective ownership and economic equality.
socialism.definition: Socialism is a set of ideas emphasizing social or collective ownership of the means of production and the reduction of economic inequality.
socialism.history: It arose in the wake of the 19th-century Industrial Revolution and in reaction to its inequalities.
criticisms: Possible concentration of power in the state
examples: Socialist parties and welfare policies in Europe
misconceptions: Socialism is the same as communism; in fact it spans a wide range from democratic to revolutionary tendencies.

social-democracy.title: Social democracy
social-democracy.summary: Combining a free market with a welfare state and democracy.
social-democracy.definition: Social democracy seeks to pair a market economy with extensive welfare policies, social protection and parliamentary democracy.
social-democracy.history: It evolved in Europe, especially the Scandinavian countries, in the late 19th and the 20th century.
advantages: Balance between economic growth and social justice
criticisms: Challenge of funding welfare in the long run
misconceptions: Social democracy means full socialism; but it retains the free market.

communism.title: Communism
communism.summary: The ideal of a classless society with communal ownership.
communism.definition: Communism is an idea aimed at a classless society without private ownership of the means of production, where resources are managed communally.
communism.history: It took shape from the ideas of Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels in the 19th century and led to revolutions in the 20th.
criticisms: In practice it has often led to concentrated power and repression
misconceptions: Communism and socialism are the same; communism is a more idealized and radical form.

fascism.title: Fascism
fascism.definition: Fascism is an authoritarian ideology that emphasizes extreme nationalism, a powerful leader, suppression of opponents and the supremacy of the nation or race.
fascism.history: It emerged in interwar Europe (the 1920s and 1930s).
advantages: (From a purely historical-analytical view) large-scale national and propaganda mobilization
criticisms: Suppression of freedom and human rights
examples: Historical regimes studied in political science
misconceptions: Fascism is merely the 'economic far right'; but its core component is authoritarian nationalism.

anarchism.title: Anarchism
anarchism.summary: Opposition to coercive authority and state hierarchy.
anarchism.definition: Anarchism is a body of thought that opposes the coercive authority of the state and seeks a society based on voluntary cooperation and self-governance.
anarchism.history: It took shape in the 19th century with thinkers such as Proudhon and Bakunin.
criticisms: Doubts about feasibility in large societies
Risk of disorder in the absence of institutions
misconceptions: Anarchism means chaos; in fact it refers to order without coercive authority.

secularism.title: Secularism
secularism.summary: Separation of the institution of religion from the state.
secularism.definition: Secularism is the principle of separating religion from government, holding that public institutions should be run independently of religious authority while freedom of belief is guaranteed.
secularism.history: It is rooted in the Enlightenment and is expressed in the French constitution (laïcité) and in the United States.
advantages: State neutrality among religions and beliefs
Protection of religious freedom and conscience
criticisms: Some see it as conflicting with the public role of religion
Defining the line between religion and state is not always easy
misconceptions: Secularism means hostility to religion; in fact it refers to state neutrality, not the denial of belief.

capitalism.title: Capitalism
capitalism.summary: An economy based on private ownership and free markets.
capitalism.definition: Capitalism is an economic system in which the means of production are privately owned, and prices, production and distribution are set through free markets and competition.
capitalism.history: It spread from the 18th- and 19th-century Industrial Revolution; Adam Smith is one of its key theorists.
criticisms: Little attention to social and environmental costs
misconceptions: Capitalism means no regulation at all; in fact most economies mix markets and regulation.

welfare-state.title: Welfare state
welfare-state.summary: The state's role in providing welfare and public services.
welfare-state.definition: A welfare state is a model in which the government takes major responsibility for citizens' education, health, pensions and social protection.
welfare-state.history: It began in late 19th-century Germany and expanded across Europe after the Second World War.
misconceptions: A welfare state means socialism; but it is also compatible with a market economy.

federalism.title: Federalism
federalism.summary: Dividing power between a central government and local units.
federalism.definition: Federalism is a system in which power is divided between a central government and regional units (states), each level holding its own authority.
federalism.history: Its modern model took shape with the United States Constitution (1787).
misconceptions: Federalism means separatism; in fact it is a way to maintain unity while preserving diversity.

unitary-state.title: Unitary state
unitary-state.summary: Concentration of power in the central government.
unitary-state.definition: In a unitary state, the main power rests with the central government, and local units derive their authority from the center, which can alter them.
unitary-state.history: It is the most common form of state organization in the world and is rooted in centralized European nation-states.
misconceptions: A unitary state means no local government; in fact local governments exist, but their power derives from the center.

map.title: Systems of government around the world
map.subtitle: Hover over or tap a country to see its system of government and key facts.
map.hoverHint: Hover over countries to reveal their details.
map.tapHint: Tap a country to reveal its details.
map.selectPromptDesc: Pick a country on the map to see its system of government, capital, population and region.
map.disclaimer: Figures are approximate and for educational purposes. System classifications are high-level and neutral.

presidential.label: Presidential republic
presidential.desc: The president is both head of state and head of government, elected separately from parliament.

semi-presidential.label: Semi-presidential republic
semi-presidential.desc: Executive power is shared between an elected president and a prime minister accountable to parliament.

parliamentary-republic.label: Parliamentary republic
parliamentary-republic.desc: The executive emerges from and is accountable to parliament; the head of state is largely ceremonial.

constitutional-monarchy.label: Constitutional monarchy
constitutional-monarchy.desc: A monarch symbolizes the nation while real power rests with parliament and an elected government.

absolute-monarchy.label: Absolute monarchy
absolute-monarchy.desc: Most power is concentrated in the monarch, with no serious constitutional limits.

one-party.label: One-party state
one-party.desc: A single party holds political power.

theocratic.label: Theocratic republic
theocratic.desc: Religious principles play a central role in the structure of government and law.

directorial.label: Directorial republic
directorial.desc: Executive power is held by a collective council rather than a single person.

provisional.label: Provisional / transitional
provisional.desc: The government is in a transitional or interim phase.

territory.label: Dependent territory
territory.desc: A dependent or autonomous part of another country.

disputed.label: Disputed status
disputed.desc: Its political status or recognition is contested.

none.label: No permanent government
none.desc: A territory with no permanent population or national government.

shareSite.title: Share Qotbnama
shareSite.subtitle: Introduce this educational tool to others — on any social network or messenger.
shareSite.description: Qotbnama is a free, neutral educational tool: take a short quiz to discover your approximate political orientation and learn about the world's political systems and ideologies — no sign-up, fully private.
shareSite.shareMessage: 🧭 Qotbnama | Know your ideas better — take a short quiz to discover your approximate political orientation and explore the world's political systems. Free, neutral and no sign-up:
shareSite.platformsHint: Pick a platform to share on:

share.title: Share your result
share.desc: Share your result with others or save it as an image.
share.shareText: My political orientation on Qotbnama: {{quadrant}}. Try it yourself!

footer.disclaimer: Qotbnama is a neutral educational tool and provides no scientific diagnosis.
footer.privacy: All data is processed locally on your own device.
footer.rights: All rights reserved to the user.
footer.contactDesc: Feedback, ideas or collaboration? I'd love to hear from you.
footer.methodology: The Qotbnama questionnaire is built on internationally recognized methodologies and instruments: the Political Compass, the Nolan Chart, the World Values Survey (WVS), the European Social Survey (ESS) and Pew Research political values — and follows standard questionnaire-design principles from comparative politics and political psychology (neutral wording, one concept per item, balanced keying).

notFound.title: Page not found
notFound.desc: The address you were looking for does not exist.

history.title: Your Previous Assessments
history.subtitle: {{count}} previous attempt(s) on this device
history.movedAuth: social axis moved {{val}} pts toward authoritarian
history.movedLib: social axis moved {{val}} pts toward libertarian
history.privacyNote: This history is stored only on this device and never shared.

mapPreview.title: Political World View
mapPreview.subtitle: See which political systems countries around the world use and find your place on the global map.
